did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions as benign or malignant (melanoma detection). The methodology includes CNN layers (convolution, pooling, fully connected) and reports results such as recall (0.84), precision (0.8325), and F1 score (0.8325).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for skin pathology detection. It details a specific methodology including data preprocessing, model architecture (convolution, pooling, fully connected layers), and training on the ISIC dataset. It reports original results including recall (0.84), precision (0.8325), and F1 score (0.8325). Although published in a conference proceedings (ISPCC 2021), it contains original research results rather than being a review or survey.

title: Skin Pathology Detection Using Artificial Intelligence
journal: 2021 6th International Conference on Signal Processing, Computing and Control (ISPCC)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to automatically detect and classify skin pathology, specifically distinguishing between benign and malignant melanoma. The researchers utilized dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset, filtering for labeled benign and malignant cases while excluding uncertain samples. The methodology involved preprocessing the images, including resizing and converting to grayscale, before feeding them into a CNN architecture comprising convolution, pooling, and fully connected layers. The model was evaluated using standard metrics including recall, precision, and F1 score. The key results demonstrated a recall of 0.84, a precision of 0.8325, and an F1 score of 0.8325. The authors conclude that this CNN-based framework can effectively assist dermatologists in the rapid and accurate diagnosis of skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting and classifying skin pathology, specifically distinguishing between benign and malignant melanoma. The dataset was filtered to include only labeled 'benign' and 'malignant' cases from the ISIC dermoscopic collection, excluding 'other and uncertain' samples. Class 1 denotes the presence of malignant cancerous cells.

The study includes benign cases as the comparator class to malignant melanoma. The dataset consisted of two categories: harmful (malignant) dermoscopic images and favorable (benign) dermoscopic images. Class 0 denotes the lack of unhealthy cells.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'benign' (Class 0) or 'malignant' (Class 1) melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify skin pathology. The dataset was filtered to include only labeled 'benign' and 'malignant' cases from the ISIC dermoscopic collection, excluding 'other and uncertain' samples. The model performs binary grouping to choose suitable classes 1 (malignant cancerous cells) and 0 (lack of unhealthy cells). Evaluation metrics include recall (0.84), precision (0.8325), and F1 score (0.8325).

datasets_used.name: ISIC dermoscopic collection
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive. The study selected images at random from this collection, filtering for labeled 'benign' and 'malignant' cases while excluding images labeled 'other and uncertain'.

use.training: Images were used for training the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).
use.testing: Test data was predicted using the trained model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are presented as point estimates for recall (0.84), precision (0.8325), and F1 score (0.8325) without statistical inference.

ISIC dermoscopic collection.notes: The paper reports results for a CNN model trained on the ISIC dermoscopic collection. The dataset was filtered to include only 'benign' and 'malignant' cases, excluding 'other and uncertain' samples. The model achieved a recall of 0.84, precision of 0.8325, and F1 score of 0.8325. The authors also note that accuracy decreases after 80 iterations due to additional neurons having a detrimental impact on the system.

architecture_details.description: The CNN consists of an input layer, unseen layers for feature isolation, and output layers for binary classification. The pooling layer performs max or min pool. The straighten layer converts data to a one-dimensional vector.

hyperparameters.iterations: Accuracy decreases after 80 iterations due to additional neurons having a detrimental impact.

classes: Class 1: Attendance of malignant cancerous cells (Malignant)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 70x70 pixels to reduce computational load and memory usage.
Converting RGB images to grayscale (single channel) to address computing power limitations and simplify processing.
Filtering dataset by excluding images labeled 'other and uncertain' to retain only 'benign' and 'malignant' classes.
Discarding images that do not have a class mark.
conclusions: A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based method for melanoma classification was successfully proposed and implemented.
The proposed framework achieved a recall of 0.84, a precision of 0.8325, and an F1 score of 0.8325.
The system is designed to assist physicians and patients in detecting and identifying skin cancer classes (benign or malignant) more quickly than standard manual diagnosis.
The framework is contemplated to potentially become the standard for skin cancer diagnosis by aiding medical providers.
Accuracy decreases after 80 iterations due to the detrimental impact of additional neurons on the system.
Preprocessing steps, including resizing images to 70x70 pixels and converting to grayscale, were necessary to address computing power limitations and reduce memory usage.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC dermoscopic collection without splitting into distinct training, validation, and testing sets or reporting per-class sample counts.

The dataset was filtered to include only 'benign' and 'malignant' cases, explicitly excluding 'other and uncertain' samples, which may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability to real-world clinical scenarios where ambiguous lesions are common.

No data leakage prevention strategies (e.g., patient-level splitting) are described; images were selected 'at random,' raising the risk of data leakage if multiple images from the same patient were included in both training and testing.
The paper does not report accuracy, specificity, or confidence intervals, despite listing them as evaluation metrics, limiting the completeness of the performance assessment.

The CNN architecture is described only at a high level (convolution, pooling, fully connected layers) without specifying layer depths, filter sizes, or parameter counts, making reproducibility difficult.

The model achieved an F1 score of 0.8325 and recall of 0.84, but the paper notes that accuracy decreases after 80 iterations due to additional neurons having a detrimental impact, suggesting potential overfitting or suboptimal hyperparameter tuning.
Preprocessing involved resizing images to 70x70 pixels and converting to grayscale to reduce computational load, which may result in loss of critical color information relevant to melanoma diagnosis.
The study is a conference paper with limited methodological detail and no statistical hypothesis testing, reducing the robustness of the evidence compared to peer-reviewed journal articles.
